content:
SCENE STEALER By Paul Thomas Anderson I used to bemoan the fact that I’d never get to work with Hum-I’d never get to work with Hum- phrey Bogart or Spencer Tracy orphrey Bogart or Spencer Tracy or John Garfield or Charles Laugh-John Garfield or Charles Laugh- ton. Then one day I woke up andton. Then one day I woke up and realized I had Benicio Del Toro. Ifrealized I had Benicio Del Toro. If I were a movie screen, each yearI were a movie screen, each year I would make a national holidayI would make a national holiday celebrating Benicio Del Toro andcelebrating Benicio Del Toro and thank him for showing up andthank him for showing up and blessing me with soul, humor, cha-blessing me with soul, humor, cha- risma, and mystery.risma, and mystery. I have had the pleasure of work-I have had the pleasure of work- ing with Benicio twice. My timeing with Benicio twice. My time with him in El Paso, Texas, mak-with him in El Paso, Texas, mak- ingingOne Battle After AnotherOne Battle After Anotheris theis the highlight of my career. There isn’thighlight of my career. There isn’t a moment from that experiencea moment from that experience that I don’t remember and cherish.that I don’t remember and cherish. My man is as funny as he is deep.My man is as funny as he is deep. He’s as wide as the world and asHe’s as wide as the world and as vast as the ocean. Working withvast as the ocean. Working with him is rewarding, hilarious, fun,him is rewarding, hilarious, fun, and thrilling. How many people fitand thrilling. How many people fit that description?!? Happy Beniciothat description?!? Happy Benicio Del Toro Day 2026.Del Toro Day 2026. Anderson is an Oscar-winningAnderson is an Oscar-winning director, writer, and producerdirector, writer, and producer PHOTOGR APH BY JUSTIN BETTMAN

LITERARY LIGHT By Imani Perry A daughter of Atlanta and a writer of the world, Tayari Jones has blazed her own path through American fiction. Her sensitive coming-of-age stories bloom into rich landscapes of Black women’s interior lives. In them, key social issues and important historical moments come alive by way of her characters’ wounds, yearnings, and dreams. Her fifth and most recent novel,Kin,is an exqui- site journey through a lifelong friendship between two girls born in Louisiana who enter adulthood at the dawn of the freedom movement. In this novel, as in her preceding books, Tayari revels in language that is both liter- ary and vernacular, and she uses her abundant gifts to deeply contemplate love in all its complexity. Standing in the tradition of civil rights organizers and writers of the late 20th century Black women’s literary renaissance, she wears these inheritances like a crown. Tayari is building a legacy while nurturing her fellow writers and dazzling her readers with grace, charm, poise, and a brilliant smile. Perry is a professor at Harvard and National Book Award–winning author K A H A N : G E T T Y I M A G E S/S I R I U S X M ; J O N E S: D E R E K W H I T E — G E T T Y I M A G E S/ T I M E ; PA L M E R : J A S O N K I M — T R U N K A R C H I V E
